## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey, this is D-Pesh and in this part of the graduation testing automation, I'm going to show you the actual automation. So right now I'm using a tool called Revealbot. There is a link if you want to sign up with a discount for your account. This method at the moment doesn't work with the Facebook rules engine. So you do need a tool like Revealbot that can actually change an ad name directly. Now there's two parts of how I set these rules up. There's a rule that will pause an ad when it's hit 1500 impressions and it's currently live. And there's a second one which will then do the grading. So let me just show you this one here. So I've just changed the actual account name out. So this is where you'd have your account name. So pause, add, grad test, greater than 1500 impressions. Really imaginative name as you can see. You can call it what you want, but that's just descriptive for me. This is the account that I'm applying it for. And then if I click into here, this is the setup. So I'm saying apply at the ad level and use the filter where the campaign name contains test. Because my campaigns are called grad test that are currently in test. And the ad is currently active. And it will tell you how many are matched here. So at the moment there aren't any ads currently active. We actually are in the process of cycling in new ads to be tested. So there's nothing there to pause out. So what the rule actually does is I'm telling the rules engine to pause the ad. If it's currently live and it's in the test folder, when it hits more than 1500 impressions. So as you know from previous videos, I'm hoping you've gone through the previous grad test videos. I'm aiming for a certain number of impressions. On this particular account, I'm aiming for 1500. So look at the table and decide what you need to be aiming for as well to give you enough data to make a decision. Then I'm adding to the ad name to say tested. So this ad has been tested. So it's been paused and tested. And I'll explain in the next tab why I put that. And what I also do is I push that into our Slack channel. And so we get all the notifications that ads have been paused, etc. etc. And where I have previously in the video talked about every morning, my media buys will go in and check. If we've got lots of tests going in, we've got lots of notifications, we might check more frequently in the day to look at the analysis. But right now, we just have that sent as an FYI. You can also email it. You can also just not have notifications as well. And the key thing about this is it runs 15 every 15 minutes with the Facebook rules engine right now. It's every 30 minutes. This just means that we're using our spend efficiently as well. So that's the first part, which will pause the ad on 1500 impressions and add tested to the name. Now, once that's done, you've got that operating, it's saved out. The second rule that I've got is what I call the grad testing grader rule.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

Now, what I've got set up here is to say that if the ad to cart rate at a cart cost is below $5, then it's a winner. If it's above that, it's a loser. So in here, again, I put the account name, select the account. What I'm saying is again, set it at the ad level. And this time, the filter is looking for ad names that contain the word tested and the ad status is paused. So that from the previous rules, you'll know that that will be the case for ones that have been tested. And you can see here, there's 20 ads that currently have tested and are currently in the pause state. Now, when I go down to the task, the first thing I want to do is I want to figure out if it's a winner or loser. So, you know, FYI, you can put emojis at the moment into your ad name. And if you've seen the previous video on the analysis part, this is kind of where the magic happens. So also, if you're on Mac, I don't know about PC, but if you hit control, command and space bar, and let me just see if that works right now, you can select emojis. So, you know, you don't have to have a trophy. You can see actually my most commonly frequently used emojis here as well. You don't need a trophy. It's just whatever stands out. So I use a trophy for winners and a red X for losers. You could put a green tick by prefer the trophy. So then what it does is it finds the tested name and replaces it with whatever result I've got. So the first part is firing if the results are kind of at this level. So what it says here, so I want to make sure there's at least one to add to cart. I want to make sure my cost per add to cart is less than $5. And I want to make sure there's more than 1500 impressions. So that's the only point that this rule will fire. So if that if that isn't if that rule isn't met, then then this action won't take place. So that's the first one. Then in the same rule set, I have a second one which says if the opposite is true. So if I actually had actually it's not the opposite. So if I had more than one add to cart, but my cost per add to cart is above $15. Then market as a loser. So that should be $5 there. So let me just edit that out. Slight mistake on this particular account, but I did set it up as a test just to demonstrate. So what I'm saying here is if the value is above $5 for my testing and there's been at least one add to cart, then I want it to be replaced with this message here. So it's a loser at a cart rate is above $5 $1500 impressions. Then there's a third one which catches any that has zero at a carts because I want to make sure I've got all the scenarios. So number one is zero at a carts number one is at a cart rates too expensive.

## [06:02 - 08:37]

Number two is at a cart rate is on point. And again above 1500 impressions and with zero at a carts, then markets loser. But the cool thing is you can tell here why it was paused out. So that really helps your analysis. And then again, as I mentioned before, I've got a notification into into Slack. Now, what I've actually done here is I don't mark this as a 15 minute check or even a 30 minute. I actually put this as three hours. And there's a reason for this because what I actually noticed is when I was testing, there were cases where at a carts would come in later on. So after the 1500 impressions, it would get paused out sometimes to say, you know, at a cart rates above $5. And then like an hour later, the at a cart rate would actually drop to below $5. And actually it turned out to be a good ad. So because of that, we actually get it checking every three hours. So I'm kind of okay with it going, you know, some way above 1500 impressions. And you may have seen in the previous video, one of the ads had 1800 impressions. I'm cool with that. You know, it's just spending a couple of dollars more, whatever it is, it's fine. But at least I can make a better judgment on whether the performance has been good or not. So that's literally it. So these rules, when they're in play, the first one will pause the ad at 1500 impressions and put the word test in. The second rule will then look for the word tested and the ad status being paused. And then based on different rules, it will then add the notification into the ad name that allows me to run my reports. And I can quickly pull out the losers and winners. Now, if you're wondering, you know, what should my at a cart rate be? Should it even be at a cart? Should it be purchased? There's a separate note below this video to help you determine what it should be. But this video is to demonstrate how I set up the rules to be able to come up with my reporting, which you saw in the previous ad, previous video, to be able to come up with this terminology. So that's pretty much it. It's the graduation testing automation method. For me, this has saved so much time. And I'm pretty confident once you get this process in place, it will ease your burden for testing. You don't have to sit there and analyze what this was. This is the winner was a loser. And for me, this is easier for me to analyze, but it also helps me to train my media buyers up. And I'm hoping it helps to train you guys up on what kind of performance you're looking for. And actually take some burden away from the analysis as well.
